title: What Do Blue Light Glasses Do? How They Filter Light and Who Benefits
description: Understand exactly what blue light glasses do to incoming light, how filtering works at different levels, and whether the effect is meaningful for your eyes.
tldr: Blue light glasses use coatings or lens materials that selectively absorb or reflect blue light wavelengths (380–500nm) before they reach your eyes. Clear lenses filter 10–20%, amber lenses filter 50–80%. The filtering reduces blue light exposure but isn't proven to reduce eye strain — its main supported benefit is for sleep.

definition.text: Blue light filtering is the selective reduction of blue light wavelengths (380–500nm) by eyeglass lenses through absorption (tinted lens material) or reflection (multi-layer coatings that bounce blue wavelengths away from the eye).

sections.h2: How Blue Light Filtering Actually Works
sections.content:
Blue light glasses use one of two methods to reduce the blue light reaching your eyes:

Coating-Based Filtering

A multi-layer coating applied to the lens surface reflects specific blue light wavelengths. This is how most clear 'blue light' lenses work. You'll notice a blue-purple reflection on the lens surface — that's the blue light being bounced away instead of passing through. These coatings typically filter 10–20% of blue light.

Material-Based Filtering

The lens material itself is tinted to absorb blue light wavelengths. Yellow tints absorb 30–50%, amber/orange tints absorb 50–80%. The filtering is more effective but visibly alters colour perception. Some modern lens materials (like Zeiss BlueGuard) embed blue light absorption into the lens without a noticeable tint.

This is one of the most common questions that comes up during eye exams, and the answer often depends on individual factors that only a professional assessment can evaluate. What works for one person may not be ideal for another — your prescription, daily activities, facial anatomy, and budget all play a role in determining the best approach.

sections.h2: What Blue Light Glasses Do to Your Vision
sections.content:
The filtering effect varies dramatically by lens type:

Lens TypeWhat You NoticeBlue Light Blocked
Clear (coated)Subtle blue reflection on lens; vision appears normal10–20%
Slight yellow tintWarm colour shift; whites look slightly yellow30–50%
Amber/orangeEverything has warm orange cast; colours altered50–80%
Red/dark amberStrong colour distortion; not for daytime use90%+

For daytime use, clear or very subtle tint is the practical choice — anything stronger interferes with colour accuracy for work, driving, and daily activities. The heavier tints are designed for evening use only.

sections.h2: What Blue Light Glasses Do for Eye Strain
sections.content:
Despite widespread marketing claims, blue light glasses have not been shown to meaningfully reduce digital eye strain in clinical studies. The 2023 Cochrane Review (17 studies, the most comprehensive analysis to date) found no significant benefit.

Digital eye strain comes from how you use your eyes (sustained focus, reduced blinking, glare), not from the specific wavelengths of light hitting them. Filtering out some blue light doesn't change the focusing demand on your eye muscles.

More effective approaches for screen comfort include anti-reflective coated lenses (which reduce glare), the 20-20-20 rule, and making sure your prescription is current.

sections.h2: What Blue Light Glasses Do for Sleep
sections.content:
This is the area with genuine scientific support. Blue light in the 460–490nm range tells your brain 'it's daytime' by suppressing melatonin production. When you use screens in the evening, you're essentially sending a wake-up signal at the exact time your body wants to wind down.

Amber-tinted glasses worn 2–3 hours before bed have shown measurable improvements in sleep quality across multiple studies. They work by blocking enough blue light to allow your natural melatonin production to kick in on schedule.

Clear blue light glasses (the kind most people buy) filter too little blue light to meaningfully affect melatonin. For sleep benefits, you need amber or orange tints that block 50%+ of blue light.

sections.h2: Who Should Consider Blue Light Glasses?
sections.content:
• Evening screen users with sleep difficulty: The strongest use case. Amber-tinted glasses 2–3 hours before bed can help.
  • Prescription wearers who want a minor add-on: Adding blue light coating to your prescription lenses is inexpensive and won't hurt. Just don't expect it to solve eye strain on its own. Ask about lens add-ons at Charm Optical.
  • People who find the slight tint subjectively comfortable: If they reduce perceived brightness and you feel better, that's a valid personal benefit even if studies don't show a group-level effect.

Who probably won't benefit: people with eye strain seeking a single fix (address ergonomics and prescription first), children (outdoor time and screen limits matter more), and anyone expecting protection from long-term eye damage (not supported by current evidence).

If you're experiencing any of these signs regularly, it's worth getting a professional assessment rather than guessing. Many vision problems develop gradually — your brain compensates for declining vision so effectively that you may not notice the change until it's significant. A comprehensive eye exam can detect issues that self-assessment misses, including early signs of conditions like glaucoma and macular degeneration that have no symptoms in their early stages.

The Canadian Association of Optometrists recommends eye exams every 1-2 years for adults. Children should be examined annually since vision problems can significantly impact learning and development. In Alberta, children under 18 receive coverage through Alberta Health. Adults with diabetes or a family history of eye disease should also get annual exams. Book an appointment at Charm Optical — they accept most Alberta insurance providers through direct billing.

Your eyes are one of the most important tools you have, and they deserve proper care and correction. The technology behind modern eyewear has advanced remarkably in recent years — from digital freeform progressive lenses that customize the optics to your exact wearing position, to lightweight titanium frames that weigh less than a coin, to anti-reflective coatings that virtually eliminate glare. Understanding these options empowers you to make better choices about your eye health and get glasses that genuinely improve your quality of life rather than just meeting a basic need.

Regular eye exams are the foundation of good vision care. The Canadian Association of Optometrists recommends exams every one to two years for adults and annually for children, seniors over 65, and anyone with diabetes or a family history of eye disease. A comprehensive exam does far more than measure your prescription — your optometrist checks the health of your retina, optic nerve, and blood vessels, catching conditions like glaucoma and macular degeneration in their earliest stages. Many serious eye conditions have no noticeable symptoms until significant damage has occurred, making preventive exams essential.

The best next step is always a professional eye exam. Your optometrist can evaluate your specific needs and recommend the right combination of lenses, coatings, and frames for your lifestyle and budget. In Alberta, most employer insurance plans cover comprehensive eye exams, and children under 18 and adults over 65 receive coverage through Alberta Health.

faqs.q: Do blue light glasses actually do anything?
faqs.a: They physically filter blue light from reaching your eyes — that part is real. Whether this filtering produces meaningful benefits depends on your goal. For sleep: amber-tinted lenses show real benefit. For eye strain: current evidence says no significant effect.

faqs.q: What percentage of blue light should glasses block?
faqs.a: For sleep improvement, 50%+ (amber tint) is recommended based on research. For general daytime use, 10–20% (clear) is the most practical since it doesn't distort colours. The right level depends on your primary goal.

faqs.q: Can I wear blue light glasses all day?
faqs.a: Clear blue light lenses are fine for all-day wear. Amber or orange-tinted lenses distort colour perception and are better suited for evening use only. There are no health risks to wearing blue light lenses during the day.

faqs.q: Are blue light glasses better than night mode on my phone?
faqs.a: Night mode (which shifts screen colour temperature warm) achieves a similar effect for free. Blue light glasses have the advantage of filtering all blue light in your environment, not just from one screen. For most people, night mode is a fine starting point.

faqs.q: What lens material is best for strong prescriptions?
faqs.a: High-index lenses (1.67 or 1.74) are best for strong prescriptions because they bend light more efficiently, resulting in thinner and lighter lenses. Standard plastic (CR-39) works well for mild prescriptions. Your optician at Charm Optical can recommend the ideal index based on your prescription strength.

faqs.q: How often should I replace my lenses?
faqs.a: Most optometrists recommend updating your prescription every 1-2 years. Lens coatings (anti-reflective, scratch-resistant) degrade over time even if your prescription hasn't changed. If you notice scratches, haze, or peeling, it's time for new lenses. Book an eye exam at Charm Optical to check your current prescription.
